explicit_text: I was born in Norwalk, California, in 1926, where my family lived on a farm. Growing up there, I learned the values of hard work and community through truck farming. Norwalk was the starting point of my life journey before the upheavals of World War II changed everything for us.
implicit_text: I was born in a small farming community in California in 1926, where my family lived on a farm. Growing up there, I learned the values of hard work and community through truck farming. That town was the starting point of my life journey before the upheavals of World War II changed everything for us.

explicit_text: My family originally came from Japan, and although we lived in California, our heritage was always a part of who we were. My parents practiced Buddhism, reflecting our Japanese roots, even as we adapted to life in America. This cultural background influenced my upbringing and identity throughout my life.

explicit_text: Although my parents were Buddhists, we also attended a Quaker church in Whittier, California. This experience exposed me to different spiritual and community values during my childhood. The Quaker emphasis on peace and community shaped part of my worldview growing up.

This experience exposed me to different spiritual and community values during my childhood. The emphasis on peace and community from that group shaped part of my worldview growing up.

explicit_text: In Whittier, California, I attended a Quaker church with my family, which was an important part of my early life. This place connected me to a community that was different from my Buddhist family background. Whittier was a setting where I learned about diverse beliefs and community support.

explicit_text: World War II was a very difficult time for my family and me. My father was interned, and we were sent from the Santa Anita Assembly Center to Arkansas, which forced us to adjust from farm life to living in a camp. This experience deeply affected my life and shaped my resilience and perspective.

This experience deeply affected my life and shaped my resilience and perspective.

explicit_text: During World War II, my family was first sent to the Santa Anita Assembly Center before being relocated to Arkansas. The center was a temporary place where we had to live under difficult conditions, marking the beginning of our internment experience. It was a stark change from our farm life in California.

explicit_text: After the Santa Anita Assembly Center, my family was relocated to Arkansas during World War II. Living there was a major adjustment from our previous life on a California farm. This period was challenging but also a significant chapter in my family's history.

explicit_text: After the war, I attended Milwaukee State Teachers College, which was an important step in my education. It was there that I learned more about art and teaching, which influenced my later work in art education and community projects. This college helped me grow both academically and artistically.

explicit_text: I heard about Black Mountain College while I was at Milwaukee State Teachers College, and it inspired me greatly. The college was known for its experimental approach to art and education, which motivated me to explore art further. This inspiration even led me to visit Mexico to deepen my artistic experiences.

explicit_text: Inspired by Black Mountain College, I traveled to Mexico to explore art more deeply. This trip broadened my artistic horizons and influenced my development as an artist. Mexico was a place where I could immerse myself in new cultural and artistic experiences.

explicit_text: In San Francisco, I became deeply involved in art education and community projects. I worked with schools, parents, and teachers to create murals, mosaics, and gardens, involving people of all ages. San Francisco was the place where I could combine my roles as an artist, mother, and community leader.

explicit_text: I served on the San Francisco Arts Commission, where I helped advocate for public art and support for artists. This role allowed me to influence how art was integrated into public spaces and government projects. It was rewarding to contribute to the city's cultural life through this commission.

Through this involvement, I helped develop programs that funded public artworks and encouraged community participation. It was important to me to support artists and bring art into everyday life.

explicit_text: One of the public art projects I worked on was the Hyatt Fountain at the Hyatt Regency San Francisco. This commission was part of my efforts to integrate art into public and commercial spaces. It was fulfilling to see art become a part of the city's environment in such a visible way.

explicit_text: I promoted the Two Percent for Art program, which allocates a portion of public construction budgets to fund artworks. This program was crucial in supporting artists and enriching public spaces with art. Being involved in this initiative helped me advocate for the importance of art in community life.
